coredata.dc:title: Studies on several mixtures of PEG based phase change materials for heat transfer Applications: An experimental approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Bejan, Dana
Cojocariu, Nicoleta
Cherecheş, Elena Ionela
Minea, Alina Adriana
coredata.dc:description: This article discusses the properties of two polyethylene glycols (i.e. PEG 400 and PEG 200) together with several mixtures and their appropriateness as heat transfer fluids. The idea of the research stands on this research group previous studies on PEG 400 as heat transfer fluid and PEG 200 as a possible candidate for heat exchange processes. As far as these authors are aware, this approach of mixing PEGs for creating an improved heat transfer fluid is a complete novelty. The study compares the properties of six fluids in terms of differential thermal analysis, thermogravimetry, derivative thermogravimetry and differential scanning calorimetry. Plus, the thermal conductivity and the isobaric heat capacity were experimentally measured, while viscosity was estimated as an indication for the change in thermal transport. Experimental data were analyzed comparatively and were discussed in the context of the state of the art. Results indicated that mixtures between PEG 400 and PEG 200 can be a viable solution for heat transfer fluids, combining the advantages of the two polyethylene glycols and minimizing each PEG drawbacks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEG 200
PEG 400
PEG mixtures
DSC analysis
Thermal stability
Thermal conductivity

Corporation for National and Community Service

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digitalization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant of the Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digitalization, CNCS - UEFISCDI Romania , project PN-IV-P1-PCE-2023-0171, number 18PCE/08 .01.2025 within PNCDI IV . Dr. Bejan acknowledges the support from the Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digitization , CNCS / CCCDI \u2014 UEFISCDI Romania grant, project number PN-IV-P8-8 .1- PRE - HE-ORG-2023-0048 , within PNCDI IV .

The experimental results were collected firstly for PEG 200, followed by the mixtures and compared with PEG 400 previous tests. The discussion is a systematic one, on each parameter, excepting the thermophysical properties discussion that is performed considering the influence on all properties.This work was supported by a grant of the Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digitalization, CNCS-UEFISCDI Romania, project PN-IV-P1-PCE-2023-0171, number 18PCE/08.01.2025 within PNCDI IV. Dr. Bejan acknowledges the support from the Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digitization, CNCS/CCCDI\u2014UEFISCDI Romania grant, project number PN-IV-P8-8.1-PRE-HE-ORG-2023-0048, within PNCDI IV.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DSC curves of PEG 200 at heating rate 10 K min−1, in inert gas (N2); Inset: the glass transition peaks.
DSC curves of L2 mixture at heating rate 10 K min−1, in inert gas (N2); Inset: the glass transition peaks.
DSC curves of L3 mixture at heating rate 10 K min−1, in inert gas (N2).
DSC curves of L4 mixture at heating rate 10 K min−1, in inert gas (N2); Inset: the glass transition peak.
DTA analysis of PEG 200, PEG 400 and all mixtures.
TG curves of PEG 200, PEG 400 and all mixtures at heating rate 10 K min−1, in inert gas (N2); Inset: the T endset interval.
DTG curves of PEG 200, PEG 400 and all mixtures at heating rate 10 K min−1, in inert gas (N2).
Thermal conductivity experimental results.
Isobaric heat capacity experimental results.
Thermal conductivity variation with temperature of PEG 200 and two of the mixtures.
Isobaric heat capacity variation with temperature of PEG 200 and two of the mixtures.
Density experimental results.
Viscosity theoretical results.
Thermophysical properties of the PEG 200 + water mixture.
PEG 200 and PEG 400 characteristics, as per manufacturer.
Manufactured samples.

row.entry: Mixture composition, in molar mass fraction
Total mass of the sample, g

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis: glass transition (Tg), melting (Tm), and crystallization (Tc) temperatures of pure PEGs and mixtures.
Thermogravimetric data comprising the main degradation steps, as well as weight loss (%) and the amount of the analyzed samples (mg).
Thermophysical properties of water, PEG 400 and PEG 200, at ambient temperature (i.e. 293.15 K).
Viscosity estimation for the mixtures – differences between analytical models.

head.ce:title: Studies on several mixtures of PEG based phase change materials for heat transfer Applications: An experimental approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Advanced Research in Bionanoconjugates and Biopolymers, Petru Poni Institute of Macromolecular Chemistry, Iasi, Romania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Advanced Research in Bionanoconjugates and Biopolymers
Petru Poni Institute of Macromolecular Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre of Advanced Research in Bionanoconjugates and Biopolymers, Petru Poni Institute of Macromolecular Chemistry, Iasi, Romania

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical University “Gheorghe Asachi” of Iasi, Romania
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technical University “Gheorghe Asachi” of Iasi, Romania

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article discusses the properties of two polyethylene glycols (i.e. PEG 400 and PEG 200) together with several mixtures and their appropriateness as heat transfer fluids. The idea of the research stands on this research group previous studies on PEG 400 as heat transfer fluid and PEG 200 as a possible candidate for heat exchange processes. As far as these authors are aware, this approach of mixing PEGs for creating an improved heat transfer fluid is a complete novelty. The study compares the properties of six fluids in terms of differential thermal analysis, thermogravimetry, derivative thermogravimetry and differential scanning calorimetry. Plus, the thermal conductivity and the isobaric heat capacity were experimentally measured, while viscosity was estimated as an indication for the change in thermal transport. Experimental data were analyzed comparatively and were discussed in the context of the state of the art. Results indicated that mixtures between PEG 400 and PEG 200 can be a viable solution for heat transfer fluids, combining the advantages of the two polyethylene glycols and minimizing each PEG drawbacks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: refers to thermophysical properties (i.e. thermal conductivity)

ce:section.ce:para: Heat transfer capability is essential for all applications that requires heating or cooling and the heat transfer fluid arises as a key apprehension for all heat transfer practitioners, consequently the discoveries in new heat transfer fluids turn out to be tremendously powerful in the latter period. The interest for phase change materials (PCMs) increased every year due to the immediate need to store heat, while some phase change materials proved their possible application also as heat transfer fluids for medium to high temperatures. In this category of PCMs the forefront position is occupied by medium to low molar mass polyethylene glycols (PEGs). This research was also expanded to nanofluids (fluids with nanoparticles (NP)) area, where there are great efforts in identifying the most stable combination between base fluid and NPs. Plus, the work is motivating since new fluids / mixtures are elaborated, as for example mixtures of different PEGs.

A brief outline of current knowledge in low molecular PEGs used as a base for nanofluids is introduced herein, outlining both advantages and drawbacks, starting with the good properties and large applications of PEGs. For example, Polyethylene Glycol 200 is a polyether used in a widespread variety of domains, including pharmaceutical (i.e. both excipient and active ingredient). Due to its low toxicity, PEG 200 can be employed also as a lubricating coating for various surfaces in aqueous and non-aqueous settings, a reagent in biochemistry and as a binder.

One common characteristic of PEG is the solubility in water, as well as in many organic solvents. This molecule is the focus of much interest in a lot of areas, including biotechnical and biomedical [1].

Among the various types of organic PCMs, polyethylene glycol (PEG) has gained significant attention due to its favorable properties as a solid–liquid PCM [2]. In the last decade, studies have focused on optimizing PEG formulations and developing novel hybrid systems that incorporate materials with high phase change enthalpy to improve the efficiency of thermal management. The incorporation of various nanoparticles, as for example SiO2 into PEG structures, have shown enhanced performance across a number of thermal applications [3]. Additionally, Cao et al. [4] studied high‑performance polyimide/polypyrrole‑CNTs@PEG composites for integrated thermal management and enhanced electromagnetic wave absorption and concluded that, compared to pure PEG, the thermal conductivity of the investigated composite increased by a factor of 2.3 by PEG encapsulation. On the other hand, carbon like nanoparticles can be integrated in both liquids and solids to create high performance materials (see Huang et al [5] for details).

It is not our intention here to make a state-of-the-art review on all polyethylene glycol since a similar study, performed by Minea [6] revealed both the advantages and drawbacks of PEG and nanocolloids with PEGs a s base fluid. Nevertheless, it is relevant to notice that little research is on low molecular PEGs nanocolloids, while PEG 200 is not as much investigated [7–14].
PEG 400 was considered as base fluid for different nanocolloids since few years ago and some results are published by Chereches et al. [7,8,10], as well as Marcos et al. [9].

Chereches et al. [10] and Chereches and Minea [11] discussed the properties of PEG 400 and these were in agreement with those noticed by Marcos et al. [9] in terms of polyethylene glycol thermal stability, as well as basic thermophysical properties.

Huang and Nishinari [15] studied also the thermogravimetric behavior of PEG 400 and noted that, the glass transition point (Tg) was not observed and that can be influenced by the water traces from material (hydrophilic material).

Molmeret et al. [16] and Oh et al. [17] reported the glass transition point (Tg) of pure PEG 400 was reported at 195.15 K, while Pielichowski and Flejtuch [14] noted that for this type of materials it is not always possible to identify correctly different transitions. On the other hand, the glass transition point (Tg) of pure PEG 400 was reported at about 232.15 K [18], but in the studies performed by this group (see [10]) was not really noticeable.
PEG 200 was studied by Yapici et al. [13] in conjunction with TiO2 nanoparticles. Authors concluded that the viscosity increased and noticed shear-thinning viscosities.

Marcos et al. [19] dispersed carbon black in PEG 200, using also PVP surfactant and studied the thermophysical, rheological and dielectric behavior of the nanocolloids. The conclusion of the study highlighted the influence of the surfactant, as well as the increase in the electrical permittivity for the most concentrated suspension, phenomenon attributed to the presence of the surfactant. PEG 200 experimental thermal conductivity (i.e. 0.188 W/mK, with deviations up to 9 %) agreed with the data reported by other research groups (see Gomez − Merino [20], Dubyk et al. [21], Sun et al. [22], and Rebrovic et al. [23]). Plus, the PEG200 DSC analysis revealed three endothermic peaks at 233, 231 and 226 K that were explained by the degree of polydispersity of PEG200.

Shetty et al. [24] debated about the green lubricating greases and considered also the PEG 200 as the base fluid, in a 50:50 wt% concentration with water, reporting data about TGA and DTG curves of the mixture, as well as the FTIR spectra. For all the samples tested, the mass loss takes place at two peak temperatures. The weight loss of the rest 60 wt% PEG/W takes place above 373.15 K. Firstly, the free and unbound water is evaporating, while the rest (i.e. 10 wt%) of water is bound to the PEG 200. The remaining water quantity is assumed that evaporates at further heating. This phenomenon and its insight is also discussed by Elbert [25] and Matlock et al. [26], based on the formation of a hydration film round the molecules of polyethylene glycol.

Karaoglu et al. [27] reported the synthesis of PEG 400 with Mn oxide nanocomposite and performed FTIR analysis of PEG 400, observing that the vibrations of ions in the crystal lattice are in the array of 1000 to 400 cm−1.

As it was noticed from state of the art, most of the studies presented an increase in thermal conductivity, revealing the potential of nanocolloids based on polyethylene glycol. Nevertheless, experimental interpretation of thermophysical properties (i.e. viscosity, isobaric specific heat) together with the phase change transition are mandatory to be discussed for a complete description of the potential of these new heat transfer fluids.

This particular study aims at investigation of PEG 400 and PEG 200, as well as their mixtures as possible heat transfer fluids for medium temperatures and as a base for future nanocolloids. The rationale of mixtures consideration was in the framework of previous studies and to combine the advantages of PEG 400 (i.e. very high thermal stability) with the ones of PEG 200 (lower viscosity and increased thermal conductivity, if compared with PEG 400. The thermal properties were discussed in the context of current knowledge, considering differential thermal analysis (DTA), thermogravimetry (TG), derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), as well as the thermal properties variation. Plus, a viscosity analysis was achieved at ambient temperature in order to get a full picture of the thermophysical properties of the mixtures. All the experimental data were judiciously debated for heat transfer applications. The novelty of this study relies on the mixtures, that were not studied before, as far as the authors are aware after a careful scrutinization of the state of the art.
The experimental start point was two low molecular PEGs, as are identified in Table 1 , together with the characteristics offered by the manufacturer, Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA).

For fluids manufacturing, the chemicals were measured on a KERN ADJ 100–4 balance (Kern, Germany), which provides an accuracy of 1 mg. Further, the new fluid mixtures, as well as the pure fluids (see Table 1) were tested for thermal stability and thermal properties. The intention of the experimental programme was to investigate the fluids behavior and stability using differential thermal analysis (DTA), thermogravimetry (TG), derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC).

The thermal behavior was investigated on STA 449 F1 JUPITER (Netzsch, Germany). According to the manufacturer, the temperature resolution is 0.001 K and the balance resolution is 0.025 μg. Temperature and sensitivity calibrations were performed employing indium in the temperature interval of 303.15 – 973.15 K. The TG measurements considered 30–80 mg of every sample with a heating rate of 10 K min−1. The tests were performed under a nitrogen atmosphere at heating up to 973.15 K. Prior to TG measurements, an isothermal segment at 300.15 K was placed for 5 min to restore the atmospheric equilibrium in the furnace chamber. The loss adsorbed water molecules take place up to 373.15 K, while the PEG fluids decomposition occurs into one degradation step up to 693.75 K (Tend, K), as it was noticed also for PEG 400 (see previous studies performed by Chereches et al. [10]).

The heat of transition and the temperature of phase changes of the PEG fluids were investigated using differential scanning calorimeter (DSC 200 F3 MAIA, Germany) from 173.15 to 443.05 K under nitrogen protection at a heating rate of 10 K min−1, with purge gas flow rates of 50 mL min−1 and protective gas flow rates of 100 mL min−1. For complete freezing of PEG fluids, at − 173.15 K, at each cycle, an isothermal program was applied to the dynamic segment for 2 min in order to restore the atmospheric equilibrium in the DSC chamber. The experimental data were handled using the equipment software, NETZSCH PROTENS 4.2.

The thermal conductivity and isobaric heat capacity was measured using a C-Therm Trident Thermal Conductivity System (C-Therm, USA, 2024), equipment that has a Basic-k Module including a Modified Transient Plane Source (MTPS) sensor, in agreement with ASTM D7984. The tests are standardized by ASTM International (American Society for Testing and Materials), and the C-Therm system incorporates severe measures and experimental parameters to guarantee the highest accuracy on the market of conductivity analyzers (i.e. less than 1 %). The experimental data were collected at ambient temperature, since previous research demonstrated that there is minor variation of the PEG 400 parameters when temperature rises (see Minea and Chereches [11]).

The overall tests accuracy at ambient temperature was calculated as less than 1.5 % (i.e. including the entire experimental procedure, starting with the samples fabrication and finalizing with the property checkup), while 3 to 5 tests were performed for each fluid sample, in order to eliminate any unwanted uncertainty.

For experimental data, the data were calculated using the same technique applied by this group at all experiments, in order to get trustful data (see [7,8,10] for example). More exactly, the standard deviation:
(1)
s
( X )
=
∑
i = 1
N
(
X i
-
X m
)
2
N - 1
In Eq. (1), s(X) − standard deviation, N − the number of measurements, X − variable (for example thermal conductivity in this particular research) and Xm is average value.

Standard error, u(X):
(2)
u
( X )
=
s ( X )
N
=
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
(
X i
-
X m
)
2
N - 1

The experimental results were collected firstly for PEG 200, followed by the mixtures and compared with PEG 400 previous tests. The discussion is a systematic one, on each parameter, excepting the thermophysical properties discussion that is performed considering the influence on all properties.
The DSC analysis involved PEG 200 and mixtures (see Table 2 for details), while PEG 400 DSC analysis was previously achieved by this group (see Chereches et al. [10] for details and results).

Details of the endothermic and exothermic effects for the selected cycles for PEG 200 are depicted in Fig. 1 , while in Figs. 2-4
are the outcomes for the mixtures. 3–18 mg sample was put into an aluminum pan, sealed, encapsulated and then placed in the DSC chamber. For all measurements, the samples were quickly cooling down to the desired temperature (173.15 K) and then isothermally kept at that temperature for 2 min to ensure complete freezing of the fluids. The first heating and cooling cycle and the second heating cycle for the fluids were analyzed and outlined in Table 3 . Further on, all the results will be discussed in terms of the first heating regime, that is of interest for thermophysical properties useful for heat exchange applications.

The first heating curve for PEG 200 (see Fig. 1) indicates a glass transition at 189.35 K (i.a. a similar value was reported by Oh et al. [17]), followed by a small endotherm peak at 285.15 K that is associated with the traces of water (i.e. that are present in the samples). Further, the fluid continues to de-freeze and finishes the process completely at 443.15 K. On the other hand, it can notice that between 323.15 K and 403.15 K, a broad endotherm peak is visible in the DSC curve, identifying, thus, an increase in isobaric heat capacity. With the exception of PEG 200, where a melting point is not clearly visible, all fluids under investigation exhibit the same behavior with those of PEG 400 [10].

Fluid L2 results are in Fig. 2 and a glass transition point is noticed at 193.25 K, together with an isothermal peak at 216.05 K that is, probably, outlining the crystallization. At 254.35 K another peak appears, that appears due to the melting of the compound. Plus, at 299.58 K the mixture completely passes into the liquid phase. On the second heating regime, small differences appear on the isothermal peaks. Here, both crystallization and melting of L2 mixture exhibit one and two peaks, respectively, during the heating steps that sure reflect a different degree of structural order of molecules from new polymer, so a different crystallinity of mixture L2, effect that was also reported for other polymers such as poly(lactic acid) [28].

The curves of heating cycles of fluid L3 show a glass transition point at 190.95 K and a sharp peak at 275.45 k corresponding to melting point of PEG 400 (279.65 K). Very modest and broad peaks appear in the 200 K to 250 K interval of temperature, which clearly shows the effect of adding PEG 200 to PEG 400. In the cooling cycle, the fluid L3 (see Fig. 3) was cooled back to 223.15 K, and two crystallization point were identified, as detailed in the Table 3. It is obvious that with an increase in molecular weight, accompanied by an increase in the chain length, results in a higher density of nuclei and default an increase in crystallinity of the L3 mixture. Further on, Fig. 3 shows no major changes in the isothermal peaks which were registered.

Fig. 4 depicts the DSC curves of fluid L4. The phase transitions and melting temperatures of these binary solution appeared as two endothermic peaks (i.e. Tg of 192.35 K and Tm of 277.95 K), while the cooling regime indicates a big exothermic peak with a crystallization point at 247.85 K.

From the DSC curves of the fluid L4 can be directly deduced the higher tendencies of the crystallization attributed to the high crystallization kinetic in the case of higher molecular weights. The crystallization peak of the L4 it is evident sharper than that of the other liquids presented in this study. Increasing the addition of PEG 400 into the mixtures lead to crystal growth at higher temperatures during the subsequent cooling cycle.

Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA) is, basically, a technique in which the difference in temperature between the sample and a reference material is monitored against time or temperature while the temperature of the sample, in a specified atmosphere, is programmed.
The DTA analysis was performed to check the exothermic and endothermic effects, as well as possible mass losses at very high temperatures.

Fig. 5 presents the DTA results for all samples, including the previously studied PEG 400, in order to get a full picture and to compare the data. The first endothermic peak, identified for all fluids, appears at low temperature due to the water content in the chemicals and the second peak is mainly attributed to the decomposition of materials.
The data presented in Table 4 shows that thermal decomposition temperature varies from 679.65 K of L3 to 571.15 K of L2 (i.e. from maximum to minimum).

In terms of thermal stability, it can see that PEG 400 is stable up to 667.35 K, while for PEG 200, the temperature is lower as 590.15 K. The high concentrated PEG 400 mixtures (i.e. L4 and L3) stability is going up to 679.65 K, borrowing the behavior of PEG 400, while L2 stability is quite similar with that of PEG 200 (571.15 K). A similar phenomenon was noticed in the open literature, as was discussed in section 1 (see [19,21] for details).

The lowest stability is observed for L5 with a first point at 400.35 K, which is chemically correct, since L5 contains 0.75 W of water. Larger water concentrations in PEG 200 were added also by different groups, as Shetty et al. [24], who noticed similar phenomenon, largely debated by Elbert [25] and Matlock et al. [26].

Chereches et al. [10] discussed the TG thermogram of the PEG 400 and noticed that the temperature Tonset is around 641.15 K, where is established as the point of intersection of the starting-mass baseline and the tangent to the TG curve at the point of maximum gradient. Also, the first mass loss (i.e. 3 %) was noted at about 573.15 K which shows a high stability of PEG 400, as was also demonstrated by Marcos et al. [9]. The residual weight of 0.98 % at 972.65 K is attributed to the decomposition products. Results for PEG 200 TG analysis are outlined in Fig. 6 , where it can notice that the mass loss point is around 433.15 K, value that is comparable with the one reported by Li et al. [29]. Additionally, in L2, L3 and L4 behavior it can notice the strong influence of the presence of PEG 400 in the mixture. For the fluids L3 and L4, the mass loss rate reaches the maximum when the temperature arrives at around 670 K. It is reported that PEG begins to degrade at less than 547 K. The mass loss points for L3 and L4 are approximately equal: 498.15 K. It can be observed from Fig. 6 and Table 4 that the single decomposition temperature of the liquids increases with the addition of PEG 400, and there is an only one step decomposition behavior, with formation of volatile compounds (CO2). Due to the high heating rate employed in the TG analysis in this study, the identification of possible fragmentations or the formation of intermediate species (e.g., radical) is difficult, as demonstrated in previous reports [30].
Fig. 6 reveals also that L5 (the mixture between PEG and water) starts to drop mass at a lower temperature, at around 333.15–343.15 K, as it is the water that starts to evaporate.

As a conclusion, it can say that the most stable fluid is PEG 400, then follows, in order: L4, L3, L2, PEG 200 and L5, that is the less stable one due to the presence of water in the sample. These liquids can play a key role in thermal energy and, as showed by Cao et al [31], can be employed as a new heat transfer fluid.

DTG analysis, as is reflected in Fig. 7 , revealed that PEG 400 has the maximum mass loss rate at 673.15 K, while PEG 200 presents a maximum peak at 598.15 K. The mixtures behavior is between these 2 limits, noting that L2 has 2 maximum points: at 593.15 and 673.15 K. Correspondingly, for L4 and L3, the DTG peak indicates that it decomposes much more slowly than PEG 400. It practically indicates a slight increase in stability and a minor influence of PEG 200. This phenomenon appears due to the low PEG 200 mass concentration in the mixture.

L5 has a completely different comportment that is determined by the water strong influence. So, two maximum peaks are noticed: one related to the water and the other related to the decomposition point of PEG 200, as can be seen from Fig. 7.

The thermophysical properties experimental and theoretical results are analyzed comparatively with previous outputs about water and PEG 400. In order to get a full picture of the two chemicals thermophysical properties, several relevant are inserted in Table 5 . In Table 5 are depicted the data from different references, as well as the data measured at the debut of this experimental study. The thermal conductivity and the isobaric heat capacity of PEG 200 are higher than the values measured for PEG 400, indicating a better behavior at heat transfer. Another advantage, a more important one for heat exchange operations, is the lower viscosity of PEG 200 if compared with the higher mass polyethylene glycol (i.e. the viscosity of PEG 200 is about half of that registered for PEG 400). In the light of this comparison of thermophysical properties of the 2 PEGs, this section comprises of an in-depth discussion of the PEG mixtures. Consequently, the PEG mixtures will be firstly discussed, followed by a study on the mixture between distilled water and PEG 200.
As it was affirmed earlier, the rationale of the PEG mixtures preparation and analysis was to combine the advantages of the two low molar mass PEGs and this a complete novel approach.

Results for thermal conductivity are in Fig. 8 where it can see that the mixtures thermal conductivity values are between the two PEGs. The value for PEG 200 thermal conductivity is in line with previous reported experimental research, with a deviation of around ± 3 % (see also the values reported by different groups [19–23]). Off course, all the data are affected by the purity of the compound, accuracy of the equipment etc. Nevertheless, the data reported here were collected with a high precision equipment (see section 2 for details).
Isobaric heat capacity at ambient temperature is outlined in Fig. 9 and the same observation is made as for the thermal conductivity.

In order to get a better picture on thermal conductivity and isobaric heat capacity of PEGs and their mixture, several tests on temperature variation revealed that the thermal behavior of the PEG 200 and mixtures remains constant when temperature is increasing. The same phenomenon was noticed for PEG 400 (see Chereches et al. [10] and Minea and Chereches [11]). Fig. 10 contains the experimental data when temperature rises up to 343.15 K. The C-Therm software registered up to 130 data points for each fluid and the results revealed that the thermal conductivity varies around 0.209, 0.206 and 0.205 W/mK for PEG 200, F3 and F4, respectively. The variation is in the limits of equipment accuracy, being ± 1.62, ± 1.38 and ± 1.48 for PEG 200, F3 and F4, respectively. Results for isobaric heat capacity are in Fig. 11 , where the same trend is noticed, the variation being between ± 1.27 % and ± 1.48 %.

Fig. 12 shows the density variation, as was experimentally estimated using the mixture equation and the Getti balance for verification. Nevertheless, the enhancement is rather small, since the 2 PEGs are, somehow, similar in terms of thermal conductivity (3 % variation in the favor of PEG 200) and isobaric heat capacity (i.e. 1.6 % in the favor of PEG 200), as can be seen from Table 4. On the other hand side, the viscosity of PEG 200 is almost half of the one registered for PEG 400 (as can be noticed from the data inserted in Table 4) and that was one of the major point of interest when proposing the PEGs mixtures.
Viscosity of fluid mixtures can be estimated with several basic models that were discussed by Ramírez-de-Santiago [32].

In 1901, Lees [33] considered that the phases are spatially distributed resembling a chessboard, alternating pattern of color squares. He reformulated Eq. (3) for the viscosity of a mixture as Eq. (4):
(3)
η
mixture
=
η
1
x 1
η
2
x 2
(4)
1 / η
mixture
=
x 1
/
η 1
+
x 2
/ η
2
Where x refers to mole fraction and η for viscosity.
Eq. (4) is known as a weighted harmonic mean and was also proposed by Bingham [34].

Additionally, Lees [33] noticed that the experimental data are not very well predicted with these two equations and formulated a new correlation, as:
(5)
η
mixture
q
=
x 1
η
1
q
+
x 2
η
2
q
where q is a constant depending on experimental data, having values between 0.20 and 0.50. Later on, Kendall and More [35] proposed to use q = 1/3 and molar fraction instead of volume fraction.

On the other hand, the classic Arrhenius [36] mixing rule writes as:
(6)
l n ( η
mixture
) =
x 1
ln
η 1
+ x
2
l n
(
η 2
)
Viscosity data, calculated with the outlined equations are outlined for comparison reasons in Table 6 and Eq. (6) results are plotted in Fig. 13 .

Table 6 shows little variation in viscosity estimation regardless of the equation employed for PEG 400 + PEG 200 mixtures (i.e. the deviation is about 3–4.5 %, depending on the sample), while large differences are noticed for PEG 200 + water. The phenomenon that appears relies on the major difference between water and PEG 200 intrinsic viscosities and these values have to be further checked experimentally.

Fig. 13 demonstrates a high reduction in viscosity when PEG 200 mol fraction increases and this is a normal phenomenon since the intrinsic viscosities of the PEG 200 is with 45.87 % lower than that of PEG 400.

Combining all the measured and estimated properties it can affirm that the mixtures have clear benefits in comparison with the pure substances. Plus, if it considers also the TG and DTG outputs, it can conclude that the PEG 200 + PEG 400 mixtures surpass the drawbacks of the low thermal stability of PEG 200, as well as the high viscosity of PEG 400.

Further on, the mixture between PEG 200 and water thermophysical properties, as are depicted in Fig. 14 , clearly outlines the major influence of water, that has better properties for heat transfer (as for example: higher thermal conductivity, low viscosity).

Fig. 14 shows a decrease in thermal conductivity and isobaric heat capacity, combined with a major increase in viscosity when PEG 200 is added to water. Nevertheless, this behavior was as expected; however, this kind of mixture can work on higher temperatures, as was noticed from the TG and DTG analysis. Also, this group intention is also to test this mixture as a base for future nanocolloids, aiming to a better stability of the nanocolloid, since PEG can act as surfactants (see also [6] for details).

Discussion on viscosity of liquid mixtures is scarce in the open literature; however, several good insights were published by Schmirler et al. [37] by comparing glycerol − water mixture experimental data with a number of available equations for mixtures viscosity estimation. The study conclusion was that the logarithmic dependence was the most suitable approach and authors proposed a correction coefficient. Plus, they suggested that for glycerol – water mixtures, more accurate results are attained when using mass concentration (i.e. as a substitute for molar concentration). Nevertheless, this particular theory needs a proper justification and an intense experimental validation [37,38].

As was earlier stated, this study on PEG mixtures is the basis for future nanofluids, and our intention is to establish the best fluid base for further adding several types of nanoparticles. Our group previous studies on nanofluids with PEG 400 (see Chereches et al. [7,8,10], Minea and Chereches et al. [11] for precise details and outcomes) revealed that both carbon based and oxide nanoparticles addition goes to an enhancement in thermal properties and a reasonable increase in viscosity, making PEG 400 a viable candidate for heat transfer applications at medium temperatures (i.e. in the range of its thermal stability – see the TG results from Fig. 6). So, after careful investigation and selection of the base fluid, this study will continue with suspensions and the results will be compared with previous results on PEG 400 nanofluids.
Concluding, this research provides data support for the subsequent development of high-performance nanofluids.

The rationale of the research presented here was the need for more stable, less viscous and higher conductive fluids for heat exchange applications at medium to high temperature. Since low mass polyethylene glycols can be a viable option and this group attained good results on PEG 400, the studies were extended to PEG 200 and the novel idea of mixtures came from the balance of advantages and drawbacks for these 2 PEGs. Plus, the thermophysical properties and stability of a mixture between PEG 200 and water were also checked as a possible new heat transfer fluid. A summary of the main outcomes is:
•
PEG 400 has the best thermal stability (the first mass loss was at about 573.15 K), if compared to the investigated fluids, as was noticed from the TG analysis. The mass loss temperature of PEG 200 is lower with 32 % than that of PEG 400.
•
PEG 200 has better heat transfer properties than that of PEG 400. For example, the average thermal conductivity of PEG 200 is with about 4 % higher if compared with PEG 400. The viscosity of PEG 200 is with 54 % lower than that of PEG 400.
•
All the mixtures have better heat transfer thermophysical properties that that of PEG 400 (i.e. thermal conductivity increase by up to 4 %, viscosity decrease by up to 54 %, isobaric heat capacity increase by up to 4 %).
•
The theoretical estimation of viscosity can be employed for the PEG mixtures, but is not giving reliable results for PEG + water samples.
•
L3 and L4 mixtures have very good thermal stability, concluding that the addition of PEG 400 improves greatly the stability of PEG 200.

Concluding, 0.50 PEG200 + 0.50 PEG400 and 0.25 PEG200 + 0.75 PEG400 are the best candidates for heat transfer applications; however, the viscosity needs to be further investigated. In terms of viscosity, there are a number of equations in the open literature, but most of them are empirically determined dependencies, being valid only to certain mixtures.

Nevertheless, in the light of this experimental study, these authors can conclude that further work is mandatory to be able to draw a strong conclusion in terms of fluid capabilities (i.e. also with a focus on viscosity), especially in terms of using the liquid PEGs as possible heat transfer fluids. Further optimization of mixing ratios or development of nanofluids based on PEG mixtures is desirable in order to test the potential of PEG based nanocolloids for medium temperature heat transfer applications.

Dana Bejan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Nicoleta Cojocariu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Elena Ionela Cherecheş: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Alina Adriana Minea: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Dr. Bejan acknowledges the support from the Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digitization, CNCS/CCCDI—UEFISCDI Romania grant, project number PN-IV-P8-8.1-PRE-HE-ORG-2023-0048, within PNCDI IV.
